title: The Sacred Alignment of Angkor Wat
highlights: Suryavarman II commissioned Angkor Wat in the early 1100s, dedicating it to Vishnu, one of the supreme deities of Hinduism. Unlike most Khmer temples, which faced east, Angkor Wat faces west. This unusual orientation has provoked centuries of debate. Some scholars suggest it symbolized Vishnu, who is often associated with the west; others believe it reflected funerary purposes, with the west linked to death and the setting sun. Whatever its origin, the western orientation plays a crucial role in the temple’s astronomical alignments. The temple’s design embodies Mount Meru, the mythical mountain at the center of the Hindu universe, surrounded by  ...    Beyond the equinoxes, the temple also reflects the solar year.
